title: Use matrices to transform the plane
description: Work with 2x2 matrices as transformations of the plane.
topic_path: Using matrices to transform the plane
Use matrices to transform the plane

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this matrix transformation:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
2 & -1
\\\\
1 & 3
\end{array}\right]$

**What is the image of $\left[\begin{array}{c}
2
\\\\
3
\end{array}\right]$ under this transformation?**

[[☃ matrix 1]]
hints:
### Key understanding

We should use these facts regarding the general transformation matrix $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE a & \redE b
\\\\
\tealE c & \redE d
\end{array}\right]$ (where a prime symbol $^\prime$ indicates an image under the transformation):

- $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]^\prime =\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
a
\\\\
c
\end{array}\right]}$.

- $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]^\prime =\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
b
\\\\
d
\end{array}\right]}$.

- For any other vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y
\end{array}\right]$, we can write it as $x\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]+y\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ and then the image would be $x\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]^\prime+y\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]^\prime$.

In our case, the pre-image vector is $\left[\begin{array}{c}
2
\\\\
3
\end{array}\right]$ and the transformation matrix is $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE 2 & \redE{-1}
\\\\
\tealE 1 & \redE 3
\end{array}\right]$. Let's find the image:

$\begin{align}
\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE 2 & \redE{-1}
\\\\
\tealE 1 & \redE 3
\end{array}\right]
\left(\left[\begin{array}{c}
2
\\\\
3
\end{array}\right]\right)&=2\cdot \left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE 2
\\\\
\tealE 1
\end{array}\right]+3\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
\redE{-1}
\\\\
\redE 3
\end{array}\right]
\\\\
&=\left[\begin{array}{c}
4
\\\\
2
\end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{c}
-3
\\\\
9
\end{array}\right]
\\\\
&=\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
11
\end{array}\right]
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

This is the image we obtain when we perform the transformation $\left[\begin{array}{c}
2 & -1
\\\\
1 & 3
\end{array}\right]$ on the pre-image $\left[\begin{array}{c}
2
\\\\
3
\end{array}\right]$:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
11
\end{array}\right]$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this matrix transformation:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
-2 & 4
\\\\
3 & -1
\end{array}\right]$

**What is the image of $\left[\begin{array}{c}
-1
\\\\
-2
\end{array}\right]$ under this transformation?**

[[☃ matrix 1]]
hints:
### Key understanding

We should use these facts regarding the general transformation matrix $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE a & \redE b
\\\\
\tealE c & \redE d
\end{array}\right]$ (where a prime symbol $^\prime$ indicates an image under the transformation):

- $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]^\prime =\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
a
\\\\
c
\end{array}\right]}$.

- $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]^\prime =\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
b
\\\\
d
\end{array}\right]}$.

- For any other vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y
\end{array}\right]$, we can write it as $x\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]+y\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ and then the image would be $x\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]^\prime+y\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]^\prime$.

This is the image we obtain when we perform the transformation $\left[\begin{array}{c}
-2 & 4
\\\\
3 & -1
\end{array}\right]$ on the pre-image $\left[\begin{array}{c}
-1
\\\\
-2
\end{array}\right]$:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
-6
\\\\
-1
\end{array}\right]$

\\\\

[[☃ matrix 1]]

y

0

\\\\

This is the image we obtain when we perform the transformation $\left[\begin{array}{c}

\\\\

$\left[\begin{array}{c}

\\\\

\end{array}\right]$

\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ under this transformation?**

[[☃ matrix 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this matrix transformation:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
-1 & -2
\\\\
4 & -1
\end{array}\right]$

**What is the image of $\left[\begin{array}{c}
3
\\\\
-2
\end{array}\right]$ under this transformation?**

[[☃ matrix 1]]
hints:
### Key understanding

We should use these facts regarding the general transformation matrix $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE a & \redE b
\\\\
\tealE c & \redE d
\end{array}\right]$ (where a prime symbol $^\prime$ indicates an image under the transformation):

- $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]^\prime =\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
a
\\\\
c
\end{array}\right]}$.

- $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]^\prime =\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
b
\\\\
d
\end{array}\right]}$.

- For any other vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y
\end{array}\right]$, we can write it as $x\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]+y\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ and then the image would be $x\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]^\prime+y\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]^\prime$.

This is the image we obtain when we perform the transformation $\left[\begin{array}{c}
-1 & -2
\\\\
4 & -1
\end{array}\right]$ on the pre-image $\left[\begin{array}{c}
3
\\\\
-2
\end{array}\right]$:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
14
\end{array}\right]$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider these matrix transformations:

$\begin{align}
\bold A&=\left[\begin{array}{c}
-4 & 1
\\\\
2 & -2
\end{array}\right]
\\\\
\bold B&=\left[\begin{array}{c}
5 & 2
\\\\
-1 & -3
\end{array}\right]
\end{align}$

This is the result of composing $\bold A\circ \bold B$, with the second column missing.

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
-21 & x
\\\\
12 & y
\end{array}\right]$

**Find the missing values.**

$x=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$y=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
### Strategy

The transformation $\bold A\circ \bold B$ is what you get when you first apply $\bold B$ on a vector, then apply $\bold A$ on the image of the first transformation.

We can find $\bold A\circ \bold B$ using these facts regarding the general transformation matrix $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE a & \redE b
\\\\
\tealE c & \redE d
\end{array}\right]$ (where a prime symbol $^\prime$ indicates an image under the transformation):

- $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]^\prime =\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
a
\\\\
c
\end{array}\right]}$.

- $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]^\prime =\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
b
\\\\
d
\end{array}\right]}$.

- For any other vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y
\end{array}\right]$, we can write it as $x\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]+y\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ and then the image would be $x\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]^\prime+y\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]^\prime$.

Since we are looking for the *second* column of $\bold A\circ \bold B$, we can use the above facts to find the image of the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ under the composition $\bold A\circ \bold B$. This image will give us the missing column.

### Finding $\bold A\left(\bold B\left(\left[\begin{array}{c}0\\\\1\end{array}\right]\right)\right)$

Now that we know what $\bold B\left(\left[\begin{array}{c}0\\\\1\end{array}\right]\right)$ is, we can apply $\bold A$ to that:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\bold A\left(\bold B\left(\left[\begin{array}{c}0\\\\1\end{array}\right]\right)\right)
\\\\
&=\bold A\left(\left[\begin{array}{c}
2
\\\\
-3
\end{array}\right]\right)
\\\\
&=\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE{-4} & \redE{1}
\\\\
\tealE 2 & \redE{-2}
\end{array}\right]\left(\left[\begin{array}{c}
2
\\\\
-3
\end{array}\right]\right)
\\\\
&=2\cdot \left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE{-4}
\\\\
\tealE 2
\end{array}\right]+(-3)\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
\redE{1}
\\\\
\redE{-2}
\end{array}\right]
\\\\
&=\left[\begin{array}{c}
-8
\\\\
4
\end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{c}
-3
\\\\
6
\end{array}\right]
\\\\
&=\left[\begin{array}{c}
-11
\\\\
10
\end{array}\right]
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

This is the composition matrix:

$\bold A\circ \bold B=\left[\begin{array}{c}
-21 & -11
\\\\
12 & 10
\end{array}\right]$

So these are the missing values:

$\begin{align}
x&=-11
\\\\
y&=10
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider these matrix transformations:

$\begin{align}
\bold A&=\left[\begin{array}{c}
2 & 3
\\\\
1 & -1
\end{array}\right]
\\\\
\bold B&=\left[\begin{array}{c}
-3 & 1
\\\\
-2 & 4
\end{array}\right]
\end{align}$

This is the result of composing $\bold A\circ \bold B$, with the second column missing.

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
-12 & x
\\\\
-1 & y
\end{array}\right]$

**Find the missing values.**

$x=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$y=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
### Strategy

The transformation $\bold A\circ \bold B$ is what you get when you first apply $\bold B$ on a vector, then apply $\bold A$ on the image of the first transformation.

We can find $\bold A\circ \bold B$ using these facts regarding the general transformation matrix $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE a & \redE b
\\\\
\tealE c & \redE d
\end{array}\right]$ (where a prime symbol $^\prime$ indicates an image under the transformation):

- $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]^\prime =\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
a
\\\\
c
\end{array}\right]}$.

- $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]^\prime =\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
b
\\\\
d
\end{array}\right]}$.

- For any other vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y
\end{array}\right]$, we can write it as $x\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]+y\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ and then the image would be $x\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]^\prime+y\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]^\prime$.

Since we are looking for the *second* column of $\bold A\circ \bold B$, we can use the above facts to find the image of the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ under the composition $\bold A\circ \bold B$. This image will give us the missing column.

### Finding $\bold A\left(\bold B\left(\left[\begin{array}{c}0\\\\1\end{array}\right]\right)\right)$

Now that we know what $\bold B\left(\left[\begin{array}{c}0\\\\1\end{array}\right]\right)$ is, we can apply $\bold A$ to that:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\bold A\left(\bold B\left(\left[\begin{array}{c}0\\\\1\end{array}\right]\right)\right)
\\\\
&=\bold A\left(\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
4
\end{array}\right]\right)
\\\\
&=\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE{2} & \redE{3}
\\\\
\tealE 1 & \redE{-1}
\end{array}\right]\left(\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
4
\end{array}\right]\right)
\\\\
&=1\cdot \left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE{2}
\\\\
\tealE 1
\end{array}\right]+4\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
\redE{3}
\\\\
\redE{-1}
\end{array}\right]
\\\\
&=\left[\begin{array}{c}
2
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{c}
12
\\\\
-4
\end{array}\right]
\\\\
&=\left[\begin{array}{c}
14
\\\\
-3
\end{array}\right]
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

This is the composition matrix:

$\bold A\circ \bold B=\left[\begin{array}{c}
-12 & 14
\\\\
-1 & -3
\end{array}\right]$

So these are the missing values:

$\begin{align}
x&=14
\\\\
y&=-3
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider these matrix transformations:

$\begin{align}
\bold A&=\left[\begin{array}{c}
-1 & -2
\\\\
-3 & -4
\end{array}\right]
\\\\
\bold B&=\left[\begin{array}{c}
2 & -3
\\\\
1 & 2
\end{array}\right]
\end{align}$

This is the result of composing $\bold A\circ \bold B$, with the second column missing.

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
-4& x
\\\\
-10 & y
\end{array}\right]$

**Find the missing values.**

$x=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$y=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]

### Strategy

The transformation $\bold A\circ \bold B$ is what you get when you first apply $\bold B$ on a vector, then apply $\bold A$ on the image of the first transformation.

- For any other vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}

\\\\
y
\end{array}\right]$, we can write it as $x\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]+y\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\

\end{array}\right]$ and then the image would be $x\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]^\prime+y\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\

\end{array}\right]^\prime$.

Since we are looking for the *second* column of $\bold A\circ \bold B$, we can use the above facts to find the image of the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}

This is the composition matrix:

$\bold A\circ \bold B=\left[\begin{array}{c}

So these are the missing values:

y&=1
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider these matrix transformations:

$\begin{align}
\bold A&=\left[\begin{array}{c}
-4 & -3
\\\\
1 & -2
\end{array}\right]
\\\\
\bold B&=\left[\begin{array}{c}
3 & -2
\\\\
-3 & 1
\end{array}\right]
\end{align}$

This is the result of composing $\bold A\circ \bold B$, with the first column missing.

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
x & 5
\\\\
y & -4
\end{array}\right]$

**Find the missing values.**

$x=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$y=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
### Strategy

The transformation $\bold A\circ \bold B$ is what you get when you first apply $\bold B$ on a vector, then apply $\bold A$ on the image of the first transformation.

We can find $\bold A\circ \bold B$ using these facts regarding the general transformation matrix $\left[\begin{array}{c}

\\\\

\\\\

- For any other vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y
\end{array}\right]$, we can write it as $x\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]+y\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}

\\\\

\\\\
0

Since we are looking for the *first* column of $\bold A\circ \bold B$, we can use the above facts to find the image of the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ under the composition $\bold A\circ \bold B$. This image will give us the missing column.

This is the composition matrix:

$\bold A\circ \bold B=\left[\begin{array}{c}
-3 & 5
\\\\
9 & -4
\end{array}\right]$

So these are the missing values:

$\begin{align}
x&=-3
\\\\
y&=9
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider these matrix transformations:

$\begin{align}
\bold A&=\left[\begin{array}{c}
-4 & -1
\\\\
-4 & 2
\end{array}\right]
\\\\
\bold B&=\left[\begin{array}{c}
1 & 4
\\\\
4 & 1
\end{array}\right]
\end{align}$

This is the result of composing $\bold A\circ \bold B$, with the first column missing.

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
x & -17
\\\\
y & -14
\end{array}\right]$

**Find the missing values.**

$x=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$y=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
### Strategy

The transformation $\bold A\circ \bold B$ is what you get when you first apply $\bold B$ on a vector, then apply $\bold A$ on the image of the first transformation.

We can find $\bold A\circ \bold B$ using these facts regarding the general transformation matrix $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE a & \redE b
\\\\
\tealE c & \redE d
\end{array}\right]$ (where a prime symbol $^\prime$ indicates an image under the transformation):

- $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]^\prime =\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
a
\\\\
c
\end{array}\right]}$.

- $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]^\prime =\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
b
\\\\
d
\end{array}\right]}$.

- For any other vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y
\end{array}\right]$, we can write it as $x\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]+y\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ and then the image would be $x\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]^\prime+y\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]^\prime$.

Since we are looking for the *first* column of $\bold A\circ \bold B$, we can use the above facts to find the image of the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ under the composition $\bold A\circ \bold B$. This image will give us the missing column.

This is the composition matrix:

$\bold A\circ \bold B=\left[\begin{array}{c}
-8 & -17
\\\\
4 & -14
\end{array}\right]$

So these are the missing values:

$\begin{align}
x&=-8
\\\\
y&=4
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider these matrix transformations:

$\begin{align}
\bold A&=\left[\begin{array}{c}
1 & -3
\\\\
-2 & -4
\end{array}\right]
\\\\
\bold B&=\left[\begin{array}{c}
2 & -3
\\\\
2 & 4
\end{array}\right]
\end{align}$

This is the result of composing $\bold A\circ \bold B$, with the first column missing.

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
x & -15
\\\\
y & -10
\end{array}\right]$

**Find the missing values.**

$x=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$y=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]

### Strategy

The transformation $\bold A\circ \bold B$ is what you get when you first apply $\bold B$ on a vector, then apply $\bold A$ on the image of the first transformation.

We can find $\bold A\circ \bold B$ using these facts regarding the general transformation matrix $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE a & \redE b
\\\\
\tealE c & \redE d
\end{array}\right]$ (where a prime symbol $^\prime$ indicates an image under the transformation):

- $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]^\prime =\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
a
\\\\
c
\end{array}\right]}$.

- $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]^\prime =\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
b
\\\\
d
\end{array}\right]}$.

- For any other vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y
\end{array}\right]$, we can write it as $x\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]+y\cdot\left[\begin{array}{c}

\\\\
